schools.summary: Jacob journeying to Beersheba and offering sacrifice: 'and Israel took his journey with all that he had, and came to Beersheba, and offered sacrifices unto the God of his father Isaac' — Jacob (Israel), persuaded by the news of Joseph (45:28), setting out for Egypt with all his household and substance (the journey begun, Beersheba, on the southern border of Canaan, the place of Abraham's and Isaac's altars, 21:33; 26:23-25, the first stop, the setting for the vision, vv.2-4); Beersheba — the place sacred to the patriarchs (Abraham, Isaac, the border of the promised land, Jacob pausing before leaving it, the place where God had appeared to Isaac, 26:24, the fitting place for Jacob to seek God); 'offered sacrifices unto the God of his father Isaac' (Jacob worshipping, offering sacrifices, before leaving Canaan — to seek God's guidance and blessing on so great a step, going down to Egypt, the piety of the patriarch, not going without God, the God 'of his father Isaac,' the covenant God, the worship that drew the vision, vv.2-4) — Gill, Henry, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Israel took his journey with all that he had, and came to Beersheba, and offered sacrifices unto the God of his father Isaac
Jacob journeying to Beersheba and offering sacrifice
the patriarchs' place (26:24) on the border of Canaan
the piety that sought God's blessing before the great step into Egypt (vv.2-4)

schools.summary: God speaking to Jacob in the night vision: 'and God spake unto Israel in the visions of the night, and said, Jacob, Jacob. And he said, Here am I' — God appearing to Jacob in a night vision (as to the patriarchs, 15:1; 26:24; 28:12, the divine revelation, the answer to Jacob's worship, v.1, the reassurance about going down to Egypt, vv.3-4); the doubled name ('Jacob, Jacob') — the affectionate, emphatic calling (as 'Abraham, Abraham,' 22:11; 'Moses, Moses,' Exodus 3:4; 'Samuel, Samuel,' 1 Samuel 3:10, the tender summons, the personal address); 'Here am I' (Jacob's ready response, the answer of the attentive servant, as Abraham, 22:1; Samuel, 1 Samuel 3:4, the readiness to hear, the obedient attention, the patriarch ready for God's word, vv.3-4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And God spake unto Israel in the visions of the night, and said, Jacob, Jacob. And he said, Here am I
God speaking to Jacob in the night vision
the affectionate doubled calling (as 22:11; Exodus 3:4)
the ready answer of the attentive servant (22:1; 1 Samuel 3:4)

schools.summary: God's promise reassuring Jacob's descent into Egypt: 'and he said, I am God, the God of thy father: fear not to go down into Egypt; for I will there make of thee a great nation' — God identifying himself as the covenant God of Isaac (and Abraham, the assurance of the covenant, the God who had promised the land, 28:13-15, now sanctioning the descent into Egypt, the ground of Jacob's confidence); God's word 'fear not to go down into Egypt': God reassuring Jacob against his fears of going down to Egypt (the land of his fathers' sojourning fears, where Abraham had gone in famine, 12:10, and Isaac had been forbidden to go, 26:2, Jacob might fear to leave the promised land, or that the descent meant the loss of the covenant hope, or the dangers of Egypt, God bidding him 'fear not' — the descent was of God's ordering, the famine, Joseph's exaltation, the preservation of the family, 45:5-8, the divine sanction of the going-down, the providence over the whole Egyptian sojourn, foretold to Abraham, 15:13-16, now begun under God's hand); 'I will there make of thee a great nation' (the great promise, the seventy souls, v.27, would become a great nation in Egypt, Exodus 1:7, the covenant promise, 12:2; 15:5, to be fulfilled in Egypt, the nation born there, brought out at the Exodus, the providence — Egypt the womb of the nation, the promise that turned the descent into the means of the covenant's fulfillment) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What does God's 'fear not to go down into Egypt' signify?

sides.position: God sanctioning the descent (which Jacob might have feared as a leaving of the promised land) - the going-down of God's ordering (45:5-8; foretold, 15:13-16), Egypt the womb of the great nation (Exodus 1:7), the descent the means of the covenant's fulfillment

themes: And he said, I am God, the God of thy father: fear not to go down into Egypt; for I will there make of thee a great nation
God's promise reassuring Jacob's descent into Egypt (the crux of the covenant providence)
the descent of God's ordering (45:5-8; foretold, 15:13-16), not a leaving of the covenant hope
Egypt the womb of the great nation (Exodus 1:7), the descent the means of the covenant's fulfillment

schools.summary: God's promise to go down with Jacob and bring him up: 'I will go down with thee into Egypt; and I will also surely bring thee up again: and Joseph shall put his hand upon thine eyes' — God promising his presence with Jacob in Egypt (the covenant God going down with his people, the assurance of God's presence, as with Joseph, 39:2, 21, the comfort against all fears, God accompanying the descent); the promise — 'I will also surely bring thee up again': God would bring Jacob's seed up out of Egypt again (the Exodus, foretold, 15:13-16; Exodus 3:8, the promise of the return to Canaan, fulfilled in the nation's deliverance, and in Jacob's own body, carried up to be buried in Canaan, 50:13, the providence over the whole sojourn, the going-down and the bringing-up, the covenant secured); 'Joseph shall put his hand upon thine eyes' (the tender personal promise: Joseph, the son Jacob mourned as dead, would be with him at his death, closing his eyes, the office of the nearest and dearest, the loving son, the assurance that Jacob would see Joseph again and die in peace, v.30, the comfort to the aged father, the reunion, the peaceful death) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: I will go down with thee into Egypt; and I will also surely bring thee up again: and Joseph shall put his hand upon thine eyes
God's promise to go down with Jacob and bring him up
God's presence in the descent, the Exodus promised (15:13-16; 50:13)
the tender promise of Joseph at his death, the peaceful death (v.30)

schools.summary: Jacob and his household setting out for Egypt: 'and Jacob rose up from Beersheba: and the sons of Israel carried Jacob their father, and their little ones, and their wives, in the wagons which Pharaoh had sent to carry him' — Jacob, encouraged by the vision (vv.2-4), setting out from Beersheba for Egypt (the departure, in faith, God having sanctioned it, the journey begun in earnest, the patriarch going down to Egypt); the sons carrying the aged father, the little ones, and the wives — the whole household removing to Egypt (the family of Israel going down, the seventy souls, v.27, the company of the covenant people); 'in the wagons which Pharaoh had sent to carry him' (the wagons Pharaoh, by Joseph, had sent, 45:19, 21, the royal provision for the journey, the kindness of Pharaoh and Joseph, the comfortable removal, the providence, the great ruler's wagons carrying the covenant family to Egypt) — JFB, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: And Jacob rose up from Beersheba: and the sons of Israel carried Jacob their father, and their little ones, and their wives, in the wagons which Pharaoh had sent to carry him
Jacob and his household setting out for Egypt
the whole household removing in faith, encouraged by the vision (vv.2-4)
the royal provision of Pharaoh's wagons (45:19)

schools.summary: Jacob's whole house coming into Egypt with all their substance: 'and they took their cattle, and their goods, which they had gotten in the land of Canaan, and came into Egypt, Jacob, and all his seed with him' — Jacob and all his seed (the whole family) coming into Egypt with their cattle and goods (all gotten in Canaan, the complete removal, the covenant family entering Egypt, the descent foretold, 15:13, the beginning of the sojourn); the cattle and goods (the patriarch's wealth, 30:43; 31:18) — brought down to Egypt (the substance of the family, the removal complete); 'Jacob, and all his seed with him' (the whole house of Israel, the seventy souls, v.27, entering Egypt, the covenant family, from which the nation would grow, Exodus 1:7, the descent of the chosen line into Egypt, the providence, the foretold sojourn, 15:13-16) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And they took their cattle, and their goods, which they had gotten in the land of Canaan, and came into Egypt, Jacob, and all his seed with him
Jacob's whole house coming into Egypt with all their substance
the covenant family entering the foretold sojourn (15:13)
from which the nation would grow (Exodus 1:7)

schools.summary: The whole seed of Jacob brought into Egypt: 'his sons, and his sons' sons with him, his daughters, and his sons' daughters, and all his seed brought he with him into Egypt' — Jacob bringing his whole seed (sons, grandsons, daughters, granddaughters) into Egypt (the complete family, the seventy souls, v.27, the covenant household, the descent of all the chosen line); the sons and grandsons (and daughters) — the three generations (Jacob, his sons, his grandsons, the family enumerated, vv.8-27, the whole house of Israel); 'all his seed brought he with him into Egypt' (the whole seed, none left behind, the covenant family complete, the descent into Egypt, the foretold sojourn, 15:13, the nation in its seed entering Egypt, Exodus 1:7) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: His sons, and his sons' sons with him, his daughters, and his sons' daughters, and all his seed brought he with him into Egypt
the whole seed of Jacob brought into Egypt
the three generations, the covenant family complete (vv.8-27)
the nation in its seed entering the foretold sojourn (15:13; Exodus 1:7)

schools.summary: The genealogy of Israel's house beginning with Reuben: 'and these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt, Jacob and his sons: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn' — the inspired record beginning the list of those who went down to Egypt (vv.8-27, the seventy souls, the genealogy of the covenant family, the names recorded, as the heads of the future tribes, the register of the house of Israel); the record's importance — the names of the seventy (the seed from which the great nation grew, Exodus 1:1-5, which begins with this same list, the covenant family enumerated, the foundation of the twelve tribes); 'Reuben, Jacob's firstborn' (the list beginning with Reuben, the firstborn, by Leah, the order of the sons, by their mothers, Leah's first, vv.8-15, then Zilpah, Bilhah, Rachel, the genealogy of the tribes, the record beginning with the eldest) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt, Jacob and his sons: Reuben, Jacob's firstborn
the genealogy of Israel's house beginning with Reuben
the record of the seventy souls (vv.8-27; Exodus 1:1-5)
the covenant family enumerated, the seed of the great nation

schools.summary: The sons of Reuben: 'and the sons of Reuben; Hanoch, and Phallu, and Hezron, and Carmi' — the four sons of Reuben (Jacob's firstborn) named: Hanoch, Phallu, Hezron, Carmi (the heads of the families of the tribe of Reuben, Numbers 26:5-7, the genealogy of the firstborn's line, the record continuing); Reuben's four sons — the founders of the Reubenite families (Numbers 26:5-7, the line of the firstborn, the genealogy); the four sons (Hanoch, Phallu, Hezron, Carmi) — recorded among the seventy (v.27, the heads of the Reubenite families, the genealogy of the covenant family, Exodus 6:14; 1 Chronicles 5:3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Reuben; Hanoch, and Phallu, and Hezron, and Carmi
the sons of Reuben
the four sons, heads of the Reubenite families (Numbers 26:5-7)
among the seventy souls (v.27; Exodus 6:14)

schools.summary: The sons of Simeon: 'and the sons of Simeon; Jemuel, and Jamin, and Ohad, and Jachin, and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanitish woman' — the six sons of Simeon named (the heads of the Simeonite families, Numbers 26:12-14, the genealogy continuing, the line of Simeon); the special note — Shaul, son of a Canaanite woman (Simeon having taken a Canaanite wife, unlike the general avoidance of Canaanite marriages, 24:3; 28:1, the record noting it, the genealogy faithful even to such details); the six sons (recorded among the seventy, v.27, the heads of the Simeonite families, Exodus 6:15; Numbers 26:12-14, the genealogy of the covenant family) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Simeon; Jemuel, and Jamin, and Ohad, and Jachin, and Zohar, and Shaul the son of a Canaanitish woman
the sons of Simeon
the six sons, heads of the Simeonite families (Numbers 26:12-14)
the faithful record noting Shaul the Canaanite's son (24:3)

schools.summary: The sons of Levi: 'and the sons of Levi; Gershon, Kohath, and Merari' — the three sons of Levi named (the heads of the great Levitical families, the Gershonites, Kohathites, Merarites, Numbers 3:17, the genealogy of the priestly tribe, the line of Levi, from which Moses, Aaron, and the priesthood would come, Exodus 6:16-20); the three sons — the founders of the three divisions of the Levites (the tribe of the priesthood and the sanctuary, Numbers 3, the line of special importance, Moses and Aaron of Kohath, Exodus 6:18-20); the three sons (recorded among the seventy, v.27, the heads of the Levitical families, Numbers 3:17; 1 Chronicles 6:1, the genealogy of the priestly tribe, the line of the future priesthood) — JFB, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Levi; Gershon, Kohath, and Merari
the sons of Levi
the three heads of the Levitical families (Numbers 3:17)
the line of the future priesthood, Moses and Aaron of Kohath (Exodus 6:16-20)

schools.summary: The sons of Judah and the line of Pharez: 'and the sons of Judah; Er, and Onan, and Shelah, and Pharez, and Zarah: but Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan. And the sons of Pharez were Hezron and Hamul' — Judah's five sons named (Er, Onan, Shelah, Pharez, Zarah, ch. 38, the note that Er and Onan died in Canaan, 38:7, 10, for their wickedness, the line of Judah, the royal and Messianic tribe, 49:10, the genealogy); the record of Er and Onan's death (38:7, 10) — the wicked sons not among those who went to Egypt (the faithful genealogy, the line continued through Pharez); 'the sons of Pharez were Hezron and Hamul' (Pharez's sons, born, the record implies, before or about the descent, Hezron the ancestor of David and of Christ, Ruth 4:18-22; Matthew 1:3, the line of promise, through Pharez, 38:29, carried on, the genealogy of the royal line, the providence preserving the Messianic seed) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Judah; Er, and Onan, and Shelah, and Pharez, and Zarah... And the sons of Pharez were Hezron and Hamul
the sons of Judah and the line of Pharez
Er and Onan died in Canaan (38:7, 10), the line continued through Pharez
Hezron the ancestor of David and of Christ (Ruth 4:18-22; Matthew 1:3)

schools.summary: The sons of Issachar: 'and the sons of Issachar; Tola, and Phuvah, and Job, and Shimron' — the four sons of Issachar named (the heads of the Issacharite families, Numbers 26:23-24; 1 Chronicles 7:1, the genealogy of the tribe of Issachar, the record continuing); the four sons — the founders of the Issacharite families (Numbers 26:23, the line of Issachar, the genealogy, the names slightly varied in the later lists, Job=Jashub, Numbers 26:24); the four sons (recorded among the seventy, v.27, the heads of the Issacharite families, Numbers 26:23-24, the genealogy of the covenant family) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Issachar; Tola, and Phuvah, and Job, and Shimron
the sons of Issachar
the four sons, heads of the Issacharite families (Numbers 26:23-24)
among the seventy souls (v.27; 1 Chronicles 7:1)

schools.summary: The sons of Zebulun: 'and the sons of Zebulun; Sered, and Elon, and Jahleel' — the three sons of Zebulun named (the heads of the Zebulunite families, Numbers 26:26, the genealogy of the tribe of Zebulun, the record of the last of Leah's sons' lines); the three sons — the founders of the Zebulunite families (Numbers 26:26, the line of Zebulun, the genealogy); the three sons (recorded among the seventy, v.27, the heads of the Zebulunite families, Numbers 26:26, the genealogy of the covenant family, completing Leah's sons, vv.8-15) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Zebulun; Sered, and Elon, and Jahleel
the sons of Zebulun
the three sons, heads of the Zebulunite families (Numbers 26:26)
completing Leah's sons (vv.8-15), among the seventy (v.27)

schools.summary: The sum of Leah's descendants: 'these be the sons of Leah, which she bare unto Jacob in Padanaram, with his daughter Dinah: all the souls of his sons and his daughters were thirty and three' — the descendants of Leah (Jacob's first wife) summed: her six sons (Reuben to Zebulun) and their sons, with Dinah (Jacob's daughter, ch. 34, the line of Leah, the largest division of the family); Dinah named (the one daughter mentioned, ch. 30:21; 34) — included in the count (the daughter of Leah, the record noting her); 'all the souls... were thirty and three' (the descendants of Leah numbered thirty-three, the sons, grandsons, and Dinah, the count including Jacob himself by some reckonings, or made up to 33 with the heads, the largest of the four divisions, the genealogy of Leah's line, part of the seventy, v.27) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: These be the sons of Leah, which she bare unto Jacob in Padanaram, with his daughter Dinah: all the souls of his sons and his daughters were thirty and three
the sum of Leah's descendants
the line of Leah, the largest division, with Dinah (30:21; ch. 34)
thirty-three souls, part of the seventy (v.27)

schools.summary: The sons of Gad: 'and the sons of Gad; Ziphion, and Haggi, Shuni, and Ezbon, Eri, and Arodi, and Areli' — the seven sons of Gad named (the heads of the Gadite families, Numbers 26:15-18, the genealogy of the tribe of Gad, a son of Zilpah, Leah's handmaid, the record continuing); the seven sons — the founders of the Gadite families (Numbers 26:15, with some variation in spelling, the line of Gad, the genealogy); the seven sons (recorded among the seventy, v.27, the heads of the Gadite families, Numbers 26:15-18, the genealogy of the covenant family, Zilpah's line, vv.16-18) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Gad; Ziphion, and Haggi, Shuni, and Ezbon, Eri, and Arodi, and Areli
the sons of Gad
the seven sons, heads of the Gadite families (Numbers 26:15-18)
Zilpah's line, among the seventy (v.27)

schools.summary: The sons of Asher: 'and the sons of Asher; Jimnah, and Ishuah, and Isui, and Beriah, and Serah their sister: and the sons of Beriah; Heber, and Malchiel' — the sons of Asher (Zilpah's second son) named, with their sister Serah, and the sons of Beriah (Heber, Malchiel, the heads of the Asherite families, Numbers 26:44-46, the genealogy of the tribe of Asher, the record continuing); Serah (the sister, named, one of the few daughters/granddaughters mentioned) — included in the record (the granddaughter of Jacob, the genealogy noting her); the sons of Asher and Beriah (recorded among the seventy, v.27, the heads of the Asherite families, Numbers 26:44-46; 1 Chronicles 7:30, the genealogy of the covenant family) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Asher; Jimnah, and Ishuah, and Isui, and Beriah, and Serah their sister: and the sons of Beriah; Heber, and Malchiel
the sons of Asher
the heads of the Asherite families, with Serah their sister (Numbers 26:44-46)
Zilpah's line, among the seventy (v.27)

schools.summary: The sum of Zilpah's descendants: 'these are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to Leah his daughter, and these she bare unto Jacob, even sixteen souls' — the descendants of Zilpah (Leah's handmaid, given by Laban, 29:24) summed: Gad and Asher and their lines, sixteen souls (the line of Zilpah, the second division of the family); Zilpah (Leah's handmaid, 29:24; 30:9-13) — the mother of Gad and Asher (the handmaid's line, the genealogy); 'even sixteen souls' (the descendants of Zilpah numbered sixteen, Gad's seven sons, Asher's sons and grandsons, and Serah, part of the seventy, v.27, the genealogy of Zilpah's line) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: These are the sons of Zilpah, whom Laban gave to Leah his daughter, and these she bare unto Jacob, even sixteen souls
the sum of Zilpah's descendants
the line of Zilpah (Leah's handmaid, 29:24; 30:9-13)
sixteen souls, part of the seventy (v.27)

schools.summary: The sons of Rachel: 'the sons of Rachel Jacob's wife; Joseph, and Benjamin' — the two sons of Rachel (Jacob's beloved wife, called specially 'Jacob's wife') named: Joseph and Benjamin (the line of Rachel, the favored wife, 29:18, 30, the sons most dear to Jacob, 44:20, 27, the genealogy continuing); Rachel called 'Jacob's wife' (specially, the beloved, 29:18) — the favored wife (the mother of Joseph and Benjamin, the special note of honor); 'Joseph, and Benjamin' (the two sons of Rachel, Joseph, now the ruler of Egypt, with his two sons, v.20, and Benjamin, with his sons, v.21, the line of Rachel, the smallest, but including Joseph, the genealogy of the covenant family) — Gill.

themes: The sons of Rachel Jacob's wife; Joseph, and Benjamin
the sons of Rachel
the line of the beloved wife (29:18, 30)
Joseph and Benjamin, dearest to Jacob (44:20, 27)

schools.summary: Joseph's sons Manasseh and Ephraim: 'and unto Joseph in the land of Egypt were born Manasseh and Ephraim, which Asenath the daughter of Potipherah priest of On bare unto him' — Joseph's two sons (born in Egypt, 41:50-52) named: Manasseh and Ephraim, by Asenath (41:45, the line of Joseph, counted among the seventy, though born in Egypt, the two who would head tribes of Israel, adopted by Jacob, 48:5); Manasseh and Ephraim (41:51-52) — the sons of Joseph (the two who would become tribes, the double portion of Joseph, the birthright, 1 Chronicles 5:1-2; 48:5, the line of Joseph); 'Asenath the daughter of Potipherah priest of On' (the Egyptian mother, 41:45, the providence bringing the godly seed, Ephraim and Manasseh, even from the Egyptian marriage, the tribes of Joseph, the genealogy) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And unto Joseph in the land of Egypt were born Manasseh and Ephraim, which Asenath the daughter of Potipherah priest of On bare unto him
Joseph's sons Manasseh and Ephraim
born in Egypt by Asenath (41:45, 50-52)
the two who would head tribes, the double portion of Joseph (48:5; 1 Chronicles 5:1-2)

schools.summary: The sons of Benjamin: 'and the sons of Benjamin were Belah, and Becher, and Ashbel, Gera, and Naaman, Ehi, and Rosh, Muppim, and Huppim, and Ard' — the ten sons (or descendants) of Benjamin named (the heads of the Benjamite families, Numbers 26:38-40, with some variation, some being grandsons, the genealogy of the tribe of Benjamin, the line of Rachel's younger son); the sons of Benjamin (ten named, some likely grandsons, the lists varying, Numbers 26:38; 1 Chronicles 7:6; 8:1) — the founders of the Benjamite families (the line of Benjamin); the sons of Benjamin (recorded among the seventy, v.27, the heads of the Benjamite families, Numbers 26:38-40, the genealogy of the covenant family, Rachel's line, vv.19-22) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Benjamin were Belah, and Becher, and Ashbel, Gera, and Naaman, Ehi, and Rosh, Muppim, and Huppim, and Ard
the sons of Benjamin
the heads of the Benjamite families (Numbers 26:38-40)
Rachel's line, among the seventy (v.27)

schools.summary: The sum of Rachel's descendants: 'these are the sons of Rachel, which were born to Jacob: all the souls were fourteen' — the descendants of Rachel (Jacob's beloved wife) summed: Joseph and Benjamin and their sons, fourteen souls (the line of Rachel, the smallest division, yet including Joseph, the ruler, and his two tribes); Rachel's descendants (Joseph, Benjamin, and their sons) — the beloved wife's line (the genealogy); 'all the souls were fourteen' (the descendants of Rachel numbered fourteen, Joseph and his two sons, Benjamin and his sons, part of the seventy, v.27, the genealogy of Rachel's line) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: These are the sons of Rachel, which were born to Jacob: all the souls were fourteen
the sum of Rachel's descendants
the line of Rachel, the smallest division (yet including Joseph and his tribes)
fourteen souls, part of the seventy (v.27)

schools.summary: The son of Dan: 'and the sons of Dan; Hushim' — Dan's son (Hushim, called Shuham, Numbers 26:42) named (the head of the Danite family, Numbers 26:42, the genealogy of the tribe of Dan, a son of Bilhah, Rachel's handmaid, the record continuing, the plural 'sons' used though one named, a common Hebrew idiom, or implying his family); Hushim (Shuham, Numbers 26:42) — the son of Dan (the head of the Danite family, the line of Dan, which would grow numerous, Numbers 26:43); Hushim (recorded among the seventy, v.27, the head of the Danite family, Numbers 26:42, the genealogy of the covenant family, Bilhah's line, vv.23-25) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Dan; Hushim
the son of Dan
Hushim (Shuham), head of the Danite family (Numbers 26:42)
Bilhah's line, among the seventy (v.27)

schools.summary: The sons of Naphtali: 'and the sons of Naphtali; Jahzeel, and Guni, and Jezer, and Shillem' — the four sons of Naphtali (Bilhah's second son) named (the heads of the Naphtalite families, Numbers 26:48-49, the genealogy of the tribe of Naphtali, the record continuing); the four sons — the founders of the Naphtalite families (Numbers 26:48, the line of Naphtali, the genealogy); the four sons (recorded among the seventy, v.27, the heads of the Naphtalite families, Numbers 26:48-49; 1 Chronicles 7:13, the genealogy of the covenant family, completing Bilhah's line) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the sons of Naphtali; Jahzeel, and Guni, and Jezer, and Shillem
the sons of Naphtali
the four sons, heads of the Naphtalite families (Numbers 26:48-49)
Bilhah's line, among the seventy (v.27)

schools.summary: The sum of Bilhah's descendants: 'these are the sons of Bilhah, which Laban gave unto Rachel his daughter, and she bare these unto Jacob: all the souls were seven' — the descendants of Bilhah (Rachel's handmaid, given by Laban, 29:29) summed: Dan and Naphtali and their sons, seven souls (the line of Bilhah, the fourth and smallest division); Bilhah (Rachel's handmaid, 29:29; 30:3-8) — the mother of Dan and Naphtali (the handmaid's line, the genealogy); 'all the souls were seven' (the descendants of Bilhah numbered seven, Dan's son, Naphtali's four sons, and the heads, part of the seventy, v.27, the genealogy of Bilhah's line, completing the four divisions: Leah 33, Zilpah 16, Rachel 14, Bilhah 7 = 70) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: These are the sons of Bilhah, which Laban gave unto Rachel his daughter, and she bare these unto Jacob: all the souls were seven
the sum of Bilhah's descendants
the line of Bilhah (Rachel's handmaid, 29:29; 30:3-8)
seven souls, completing the four divisions (Leah 33, Zilpah 16, Rachel 14, Bilhah 7 = 70)

schools.summary: The sum of sixty-six souls who came with Jacob: 'all the souls that came with Jacob into Egypt, which came out of his loins, besides Jacob's sons' wives, all the souls were threescore and six' — the count of sixty-six: those who actually came down with Jacob (his direct descendants, 'out of his loins'), excluding Jacob himself, Joseph and his two sons (already in Egypt), and the wives (the precise reckoning, the genealogy summed); the wives excluded ('besides Jacob's sons' wives') — the count of the direct descendants only (not the wives, the precise number, 66 who came down, distinct from the 70, v.27, the careful reckoning); 'all the souls were threescore and six' (the 66, those who came with Jacob, out of his loins, excluding Jacob, Joseph, Manasseh, Ephraim, and the wives, the number that, with Jacob, Joseph, and Joseph's two sons, makes the seventy, v.27, the precise genealogical sum) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: All the souls that came with Jacob into Egypt, which came out of his loins, besides Jacob's sons' wives, all the souls were threescore and six
the sum of sixty-six souls who came with Jacob
those who came down out of his loins, excluding Jacob, Joseph, Joseph's sons, and the wives
the 66 that make the 70 with Jacob, Joseph, and his two sons (v.27)

schools.summary: The threescore and ten souls of the house of Jacob: 'and the sons of Joseph, which were born him in Egypt, were two souls: all the souls of the house of Jacob, which came into Egypt, were threescore and ten' — Joseph's two sons (Manasseh and Ephraim, born in Egypt) added to the count (the two that, with Joseph and Jacob and the sixty-six, v.26, make the seventy, the completing of the number); the famous total — 'all the souls of the house of Jacob, which came into Egypt, were threescore and ten': the house of Israel that went down to Egypt numbering seventy souls (the 66 who came, v.26, plus Jacob, Joseph, and Joseph's two sons = 70, the seventy, a number of completeness, as the seventy elders, Exodus 24:1; the seventy nations, ch. 10, the small seed from which the great nation grew, Deuteronomy 10:22, 'thy fathers went down into Egypt with threescore and ten persons; and now the LORD thy God hath made thee as the stars of heaven,' the providence — the tiny family that became a nation, the apparent discrepancy with Stephen's 'threescore and fifteen souls,' Acts 7:14: Stephen following the Septuagint, which adds five descendants of Joseph, reckoning seventy-five, the Hebrew, and this verse, reckoning seventy, counting differently — the direct line, the two numbers reconciled by the different basis of reckoning, the Hebrew 70 of Jacob's direct descendants; the LXX/Stephen 75 including Joseph's grandsons, no real contradiction, but a different enumeration); the seventy souls (the small beginning) becoming, in Egypt, a great nation (Exodus 1:5-7; Deuteronomy 10:22, the providence multiplying the seed, the covenant promise, 12:2; 15:5, fulfilled, the wonder of God's faithfulness) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: How is the 'threescore and ten' reconciled with Stephen's 'threescore and fifteen' (Acts 7:14)?

sides.position: The two numbers reconciled by the different basis of reckoning - the Hebrew 70 of Jacob's direct descendants (this verse); the Septuagint, which Stephen followed, reckoning 75 by adding five descendants of Joseph (his grandsons); no real contradiction, but a different enumeration

themes: And the sons of Joseph, which were born him in Egypt, were two souls: all the souls of the house of Jacob, which came into Egypt, were threescore and ten
the threescore and ten souls of the house of Jacob (the crux of the seventy)
the small seed (70, Deuteronomy 10:22) from which the great nation grew (Exodus 1:5-7)
reconciled with Stephen's 75 (Acts 7:14) by the different reckoning (the direct line, or including Joseph's grandsons)

schools.summary: Jacob sending Judah ahead to Goshen: 'and he sent Judah before him unto Joseph, to direct his face unto Goshen; and they came into the land of Goshen' — Jacob sending Judah ahead (to Joseph) to get direction to Goshen (the appointed land of settlement, 45:10, Judah, the leader among the brethren, the one trusted, ch. 44, sent as the forerunner, the arrangement for the settling in Goshen); Judah sent to 'direct his face' (prepare the way, get guidance) to Goshen — the land Joseph had appointed (45:10, the practical arrangement, the forerunner securing the way); 'they came into the land of Goshen' (the family arriving in Goshen, the fertile region of the eastern Delta, suited for shepherds, vv.34, the appointed land, the place of Israel's dwelling, separate from the Egyptians, a distinct people, the providence settling them there, the land where the nation would grow, Exodus 1:7; 8:22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And he sent Judah before him unto Joseph, to direct his face unto Goshen; and they came into the land of Goshen
Jacob sending Judah ahead to Goshen
Judah the trusted forerunner securing the appointed land (45:10; ch. 44)
the place of Israel's distinct dwelling, where the nation would grow (Exodus 1:7)

schools.summary: The reunion of Joseph and Jacob: 'and Joseph made ready his chariot, and went up to meet Israel his father, to Goshen, and presented himself unto him; and he fell on his neck, and wept on his neck a good while' — Joseph, the ruler of Egypt, making ready his chariot and going up to meet his father (the long-awaited reunion, after twenty-two years of separation, 37:2, the great ruler hastening to his aged father, the meeting in Goshen, the reunion at last); the tender scene — 'he fell on his neck, and wept on his neck a good while': Joseph falling on his father's neck and weeping there a long while (the overflowing emotion of the reunion, the son once mourned as dead, now embracing his father, alive and exalted, the long-pent grief and joy released, the weeping 'a good while,' the depth of feeling, the years of separation and longing, the most tender of reunions, the providence that brought the dead-mourned son and the grieving father together at last, a foretaste, the fathers note, of the joy of the resurrection reunion, and of the Father's embrace of the returning son, Luke 15:20, 'his father... fell on his neck, and kissed him'); the meeting — the climax of the Joseph story (the family reunited, the father comforted, the son's love unchanged by his exaltation, the providence's tender mercy after the long ordeal, the joy crowning the sorrow, the weeping turned to gladness) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

debates.question: What does the reunion of Joseph and Jacob display?

sides.position: The long-awaited meeting (after twenty-two years, 37:2) - the son once mourned as dead now embracing his living father, the overflowing emotion (weeping 'a good while'), the providence's tender mercy after the long ordeal (a foretaste of the Father's embrace, Luke 15:20)

themes: And Joseph made ready his chariot, and went up to meet Israel his father... and he fell on his neck, and wept on his neck a good while
the reunion of Joseph and Jacob (the crux of the meeting)
the long-awaited meeting (after twenty-two years, 37:2), the son once mourned as dead embracing his living father
the providence's tender mercy after the long ordeal (a foretaste of the Father's embrace, Luke 15:20)

schools.summary: Jacob content to die, having seen Joseph: 'and Israel said unto Joseph, Now let me die, since I have seen thy face, because thou art yet alive' — Jacob, having seen Joseph alive (the son he had mourned as dead, 37:34-35), content to die (the satisfied longing, the chief desire of his old age fulfilled, the peace of the aged father, ready to die, his great wish granted, the joy that crowned his life); Jacob's words — now that he had seen Joseph's face (alive, and the ruler of Egypt), he could die in peace (the longing satisfied, as Simeon, 'now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace... for mine eyes have seen,' Luke 2:29-30, the contentment of the fulfilled hope); 'because thou art yet alive' (the wonder and joy that Joseph was alive, against all the years of grief, the resurrection-like joy, the dead-mourned son alive, the peace it gave the father, ready now to die, the providence's mercy crowning Jacob's old age) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Israel said unto Joseph, Now let me die, since I have seen thy face, because thou art yet alive
Jacob content to die, having seen Joseph
the aged father's chief wish fulfilled, ready to die in peace (as Simeon, Luke 2:29-30)
the joy that the dead-mourned son was alive (37:34)

schools.summary: Joseph preparing to present his family to Pharaoh: 'and Joseph said unto his brethren, and unto his father's house, I will go up, and shew Pharaoh, and say unto him, My brethren, and my father's house, which were in the land of Canaan, are come unto me' — Joseph announcing he would inform Pharaoh of his family's arrival (the proper courtesy, and to secure their settlement in Goshen, vv.33-34, the wise arrangement, the loyal servant informing the king, the plan for the audience); Joseph's announcement to Pharaoh — his family come from Canaan (the open acknowledgment of his brethren, unashamed, the exalted ruler owning his shepherd kin, the report to the king); Joseph's plan (to present them, and instruct them, vv.33-34) — the wisdom to secure Goshen (the separate land) for his family (the loyal courtesy to Pharaoh, the providence arranging the settlement) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And Joseph said unto his brethren, and unto his father's house, I will go up, and shew Pharaoh... My brethren, and my father's house... are come unto me
Joseph preparing to present his family to Pharaoh
the open acknowledgment of his shepherd kin, the loyal courtesy to the king
the wisdom to secure Goshen for his family (vv.33-34)

schools.summary: Joseph's plan to present them as shepherds: 'and the men are shepherds, for their trade hath been to feed cattle; and they have brought their flocks, and their herds, and all that they have' — Joseph planning to tell Pharaoh plainly that his family were shepherds (their trade, from of old, the patriarchs being keepers of flocks, 47:3, the open declaration of their occupation, the wise plan, the shepherd trade, abominable to the Egyptians, v.34, would secure them Goshen, the separate land); the shepherd trade (the patriarchs' calling, 4:20; 47:3) — the family's occupation (the plain declaration to Pharaoh, the basis of the plan); 'they have brought their flocks, and their herds, and all that they have' (the family with their flocks, the shepherd life, the plan to settle in Goshen, the pasture-land, suited for flocks, and separate from the Egyptians, v.34, the providence keeping Israel distinct) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the men are shepherds, for their trade hath been to feed cattle; and they have brought their flocks, and their herds, and all that they have
Joseph's plan to present them as shepherds
the plain declaration of their trade (the patriarchs' calling, 4:20; 47:3)
the plan to secure Goshen, the separate land, keeping Israel distinct (v.34)

schools.summary: Joseph instructing the brethren for Pharaoh's question: 'and it shall come to pass, when Pharaoh shall call you, and shall say, What is your occupation?' — Joseph instructing the brethren how to answer Pharaoh's expected question about their occupation (the careful preparation, the plan to secure Goshen, v.34, the wise coaching of the brethren); the expected audience (Pharaoh would call and question them) — the meeting with the king (the question to come); 'What is your occupation?' (the question Joseph foresaw Pharaoh would ask, the question the brethren must answer wisely, v.34, 'thy servants' trade hath been about cattle,' the plan to secure Goshen, the shepherd answer, leading to the separate land) — Gill.

themes: And it shall come to pass, when Pharaoh shall call you, and shall say, What is your occupation?
Joseph instructing the brethren for Pharaoh's question
the careful preparation, the wise coaching of the brethren
the question (v.34) whose answer would secure Goshen

schools.summary: The brethren to be settled in Goshen, shepherds an abomination to Egypt: 'that ye shall say, Thy servants' trade hath been about cattle from our youth even until now, both we, and also our fathers: that ye may dwell in the land of Goshen; for every shepherd is an abomination unto the Egyptians' — Joseph instructing the brethren to declare plainly their shepherd trade (from their youth, as their fathers, 47:3, the honest answer, the plan to secure their settlement, the wise declaration); the purpose — 'that ye may dwell in the land of Goshen': the shepherd answer would lead Pharaoh to settle them in Goshen (the pasture-land of the eastern Delta, suited for flocks, and apart from the Egyptians, the wise securing of the separate land, the providence settling Israel where they would dwell distinct); the Egyptian abomination — 'every shepherd is an abomination unto the Egyptians': the Egyptians abhorring shepherds (whether from their own settled, agricultural and city life despising the nomad herdsmen; or from religious reasons, some animals being sacred; or from the memory of the shepherd-kings, the Hyksos, who had oppressed Egypt, the providence in this abomination — it would keep Israel separate, the Egyptians not mingling with the despised shepherds, the wisdom of God in settling Israel in Goshen, apart, where they would not be absorbed into Egypt, but remain a distinct people, and grow into a nation, Exodus 1:7; 8:22, the land of Goshen kept distinct, the very contempt of the Egyptians a providential hedge around the covenant people, keeping them unmingled until the Exodus, the separation that preserved Israel) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: Why is the Egyptians' abomination of shepherds noted?

sides.position: The Egyptian contempt for shepherds (from their settled life, religion, or the Hyksos memory) overruled by the providence to keep Israel separate in Goshen, unmingled with Egypt - a distinct people that would grow into a nation (Exodus 1:7); the very abomination a providential hedge around the covenant people

themes: That ye shall say, Thy servants' trade hath been about cattle... that ye may dwell in the land of Goshen; for every shepherd is an abomination unto the Egyptians
the brethren to be settled in Goshen, shepherds an abomination to Egypt (the crux of the separation)
the Egyptian contempt overruled to keep Israel separate in Goshen, unmingled with Egypt
a providential hedge around the covenant people, that they might grow into a nation (Exodus 1:7)
